sourceMovie.title: The Conjuring
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences can't get enough of 'The Conjuring' because it masterfully blends the real-life haunting of the Perron family with the unwavering determination of Ed and Lorraine Warren, especially in those nail-biting moments when the spirit's presence manifests through the eerie clap game. James Wan's ability to build tension, particularly during the chilling scene in the basement where the darkness looms just out of sight, keeps viewers on the edge of their seats, making every creak of the house feel like a personal threat.

seoTitle: 10 Breathtaking Movies Like The Conjuring (Haunted-House Next)
seoDescription: If you loved The Conjuring’s haunted-house dread, these 10 paranormal chillers deliver investigators, jump scares, and creeping dread—ranked for fans.
seoH1: The Conjuring Fans: 10 More Cases Worth Your Night
customIntroParagraphs: The Conjuring works because Wan treats the house like a character: creaks mean something, shadows have history, and Ed and Lorraine Warren anchor the chaos with empathy instead of irony. You get classical scares—doors, basements, clap games—without winking at the audience.

The ranked list below keeps that haunted-house investigator energy: malevolent presences, families under siege, and tension that stacks instead of stalling. When you want adjacent modern horror trails, follow the related links to The Unhealer, Hereditary, Get Out, or Men for more internal paths through similar dread.

shortComparison.heading: The Conjuring vs Men — key differences
shortComparison.body: The Conjuring maps clear paranormal threat onto a family under siege; Garland’s Men trades casefile scares for surreal folk-body metaphor. One wants you afraid of what’s in the basement; the other wants you uncomfortable in your own reflection.

midPageYouMightAlsoLike.anchorText: Shared Wan DNA—otherworldly pressure in the home (Insidious)
Silence amplifies the haunt (A Quiet Place)
Supernatural threat with period conviction (The Witch)

recommendations.title: The Haunting
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The deep emotional bonds and traumas within the Crain family mirror the Warrens’ commitment to helping families confront their darkest fears. The show's expert blending of supernatural terror with genuine human struggles will resonate with you just like the chilling clapping game did in The Conjuring.

recommendations.title: Hereditary
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The exploration of familial trauma and grief in Hereditary echoes the emotional weight that Ed and Lorraine Warren carry in their fight against evil. Its unsettling atmosphere and shocking moments will leave you as breathless as the spine-tingling reveal of the dark spirit in The Conjuring.

recommendations.title: The Others
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The suspenseful atmosphere and the twisty narrative of The Others engage viewers in a way that parallels the ominous feeling found throughout The Conjuring. As both films explore the concept of a haunting with depth and emotional resonance, you'll find chilling similarities in their revelations.

recommendations.title: Sinister
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Sinister (2012) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that The Conjuring later expanded. If The Conjuring resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

recommendations.title: The Babadook
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The emotional journey of a mother grappling with grief parallels the deep commitment Ed and Lorraine have towards helping families in distress. The haunting presence in The Babadook evokes a personal, psychological fear much like the dark spirit in The Conjuring.

recommendations.title: The Witch
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The palpable tension in The Witch, driven by a family's descent into paranoia and fear, resonates with the intense atmosphere of The Conjuring. The film’s exploration of faith and evil deepens the emotional experience, much like the Warrens' unwavering commitment to their cause.

recommendations.title: The Orphanage
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The heartfelt connection between a mother and her child in The Orphanage evokes the emotional stakes that the Warrens navigate in their investigations. The film's blend of poignant moments and chilling supernatural elements will resonate with audiences who appreciate the emotional depth of The Conjuring.

recommendations.title: The Autopsy of Jane Doe
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The tense atmosphere and the unfolding mystery in The Autopsy of Jane Doe create a gripping experience similar to the suspenseful investigations of Ed and Lorraine Warren. As the characters confront the unknown, you'll feel the same gripping fear and personal stakes as in The Conjuring.

recommendations.title: It Follows
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The relentless nature of the supernatural threat in It Follows parallels the ominous tension created in The Conjuring, where evil lurks around every corner. Its unique approach to fear and intimacy will engage you on a visceral level, echoing the emotional stakes found in the Warrens' story.

recommendations.title: Midsommar
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The haunting exploration of grief and community in Midsommar resonates with the emotional undercurrents that underpin The Conjuring's chilling narrative. Both films involve characters facing deeply unsettling experiences that challenge their spirit and understanding of the world.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Conjuring?
faq.answer: Try The Haunting, Hereditary, The Others—each shares DNA with The Conjuring while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Conjuring?
faq.answer: The Conjuring (2013) is best described as Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Conjuring part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "The Conjuring Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
